sections.title: The Nature of Money
facts: Money evolved from barter through shells, beads, gold, and paper to digital ledgers
Good money requires scarcity, durability, divisibility, portability, fungibility, and verifiability
Bitcoin satisfies all 6 properties of sound money better than any previous form

sections.title: Monetary Inflation
facts: M2 money supply grew from $4.6T (2000) to $21T+ (2024)
Official CPI understates real inflation by using substitution and hedonic adjustments
The dollar has lost ~97% purchasing power since the Fed's creation in 1913, ~85% since 1971

sections.title: Full Faith & Credit
facts: Fiat currency is backed only by government promises, not tangible assets
The US left the gold standard in 1971 under Nixon
US national debt exceeds $34 trillion with interest payments over $1 trillion/year

sections.title: Deflation vs Fiat
facts: Technology naturally creates deflation — more goods for less cost
Central banks fight deflation with money printing, transferring wealth from savers to debtors
Under a Bitcoin standard, prices would fall as productivity increases — benefiting savers

sections.title: The Fiat Graveyard
facts: The average lifespan of a fiat currency is ~27 years
Every fiat currency in history has either failed or been debased
Notable failures: Roman denarius, Continental dollar, Weimar mark, Zimbabwe dollar, Venezuelan bolivar

sections.title: The Pizza Dilemma
facts: On May 22, 2010, Laszlo Hanyecz paid 10,000 BTC for two Papa John's pizzas
This was the first real-world Bitcoin transaction
At current prices those pizzas cost roughly $1 billion each
May 22 is celebrated annually as Bitcoin Pizza Day

sections.title: Digital Money Before Bitcoin
facts: DigiCash (1989) by David Chaum — first digital cash, centralized, company went bankrupt
Hashcash (1997) by Adam Back — proof-of-work for email spam, cited in Bitcoin whitepaper
b-money (1998) by Wei Dai — proposed decentralized digital currency, never implemented
Bit Gold (1998) by Nick Szabo — closest precursor to Bitcoin, never implemented
RPOW (2004) by Hal Finney — reusable proof of work tokens, centralized server

sections.title: Who Is Satoshi Nakamoto?
facts: Satoshi published the Bitcoin whitepaper on October 31, 2008
The genesis block was mined January 3, 2009
Satoshi sent the first Bitcoin transaction to Hal Finney on January 12, 2009
Satoshi vanished from public communication in April 2011
An estimated ~1.1 million BTC in Satoshi-era wallets has never been moved
No one has conclusively identified Satoshi despite years of investigation
Even if Satoshi returned, they could not change Bitcoin's rules — there is no admin key

sections.title: The Bitcoin Whitepaper
facts: Satoshi Nakamoto published the whitepaper on October 31, 2008 to the cryptography mailing list

The paper is 9 pages with 12 sections: Abstract, Introduction, Transactions, Timestamp Server, Proof-of-Work, Network, Incentive, Reclaiming Disk Space, Simplified Payment Verification, Combining and Splitting Value, Privacy, Calculations, Conclusion
Key innovation: combining existing concepts (digital signatures, hash chains, proof-of-work, P2P networks) into a system where incentives align honest behavior
Proof-of-work concept borrowed from Adam Back's Hashcash (1997)
Section 11 proves an attacker's probability of success drops exponentially with each confirmation — the basis for the 6-confirmation standard
The whitepaper describes Bitcoin's privacy as pseudonymous: transactions are public, identities are private

sections.title: Bitcoin vs Other Cryptos
facts: Bitcoin is the only major cryptocurrency with no known founder, no foundation with control, and no pre-mine
Ethereum had a 72M ETH pre-mine and has changed its monetary policy multiple times
Most altcoins have centralized points of failure — founders who can be pressured or arrested
Bitcoin's hash rate exceeds all other proof-of-work chains combined by orders of magnitude

sections.title: 6 Bitcoin Myths Debunked
facts: Myth: Bitcoin is too volatile. Reality: Volatility decreases over time as adoption grows
Myth: Bitcoin is used by criminals. Reality: Cash is used 800x more for illicit activity
Myth: Bitcoin wastes energy. Reality: Bitcoin uses ~150 TWh/year vs fiat system ~260 TWh/year
Myth: Bitcoin is too slow. Reality: Layer 1 is a settlement layer; Lightning handles instant payments
Myth: Bitcoin has no intrinsic value. Reality: Value comes from verifiable scarcity and network effects
Myth: Bitcoin has no yield. Reality: Show me yield and I'll show you counterparty risk

sections.title: The Quantum Question
facts: Breaking Bitcoin's ECDSA would require ~2,500-4,000 stable logical qubits
IBM's largest quantum processor (Condor) has 1,121 physical qubits — not error-corrected
NIST has already standardized post-quantum cryptography algorithms
Bitcoin can upgrade to quantum-resistant signatures via soft fork before quantum computers pose a real threat

sections.title: Bitcoin Adoption
facts: El Salvador adopted Bitcoin as legal tender in September 2021
Spot Bitcoin ETFs approved in the US in January 2024
BlackRock's IBIT became the fastest ETF to reach $10 billion in history
MicroStrategy holds 200,000+ BTC as a corporate treasury strategy
Bitcoin's market cap is roughly 0.19% of global store-of-value assets (~$900T)

sections.title: Wallets
facts: A Bitcoin wallet stores private keys, not actual bitcoin
Hot wallets are connected to the internet; cold wallets are offline
Hardware wallets (Ledger, Trezor, Coldcard) provide the best security for most users
Seed phrases (12 or 24 words) are the master backup for all keys in an HD wallet

sections.title: Exchanges
facts: Centralized exchanges (Coinbase, Kraken) are the easiest on-ramp for beginners
Not your keys, not your coins — exchange custody means trusting a third party
KYC requirements apply to most regulated exchanges
Dollar-cost averaging (DCA) reduces timing risk when buying Bitcoin

sections.title: Sending Bitcoin
facts: Bitcoin transactions are irreversible once confirmed
Transaction fees are based on data size (vBytes), not amount sent
The mempool is the waiting room for unconfirmed transactions
Replace-by-fee (RBF) allows increasing the fee on a stuck transaction

sections.title: Security
facts: Multi-signature wallets require 2+ keys to spend, eliminating single points of failure
Passphrase (25th word) adds an extra layer of protection to a seed phrase
Never store seed phrases digitally — use metal backup plates for fire/flood resistance
Verify receive addresses on the hardware wallet screen, not just the computer

sections.title: The Bitcoin Network
facts: Bitcoin is a peer-to-peer network of ~50,000+ reachable nodes
Nodes independently validate every transaction and block against consensus rules
No central server — every node has a complete copy of the blockchain
The network has maintained 99.99%+ uptime since January 2009

sections.title: Running a Node
facts: A full node downloads and validates the entire blockchain (~550+ GB)
Running a node is the only way to trustlessly verify transactions
Nodes enforce consensus rules — miners who break rules get their blocks rejected
Anyone can run a node on a Raspberry Pi for under $300

sections.title: Mining
facts: Mining is the process of finding a nonce that produces a block hash below the target
The block reward halves every 210,000 blocks (~4 years)
Current block reward is 3.125 BTC (since April 2024)
Global hash rate exceeds 700 EH/s
Mining converts electricity into monetary security through proof of work

sections.title: The Blockchain
facts: The blockchain is a linked list of blocks, each referencing the previous block's hash
Changing any historical block would invalidate every subsequent block's hash
A new block is found approximately every 10 minutes
The blockchain is append-only — data can never be deleted or modified

sections.title: Blocks
facts: Each block contains a header (80 bytes) and a list of transactions
The block header includes: version, previous block hash, Merkle root, timestamp, difficulty target, nonce
Maximum block weight is 4 million weight units (roughly 1-4 MB of data)
The coinbase transaction creates new bitcoin as the block reward

sections.title: Difficulty Adjustment
facts: Difficulty adjusts every 2,016 blocks (~2 weeks) to maintain 10-minute block intervals
If blocks are found too fast, difficulty increases; too slow, it decreases
This self-regulating mechanism ensures predictable issuance regardless of hash rate changes
Bitcoin's difficulty adjustment is one of its most elegant design features

sections.title: Attack Vectors
facts: A 51% attack would require $20B+ in hardware plus $30M+/day in electricity
Even with 51% hash rate, an attacker can only double-spend their own transactions
The economic incentives make honest mining far more profitable than attacking
Bitcoin has survived every attack attempt in 17 years including the 2010 value overflow bug

sections.title: Transactions
facts: A Bitcoin transaction is a signed message that transfers value from inputs to outputs
Every transaction consumes existing UTXOs and creates new ones
Transaction fees = sum of inputs minus sum of outputs
Miners prioritize transactions by fee rate (sats/vByte)

sections.title: Outputs (UTXOs)
facts: UTXO = Unspent Transaction Output — the fundamental unit of Bitcoin value
Your 'balance' is the sum of all UTXOs your keys can spend
UTXOs are like physical bills — they must be spent whole, with change returned
The UTXO set (~180M entries) is what every node must track to validate new transactions

sections.title: Locking Scripts
facts: Every UTXO has a locking script (scriptPubKey) that defines spending conditions
Common types: P2PKH, P2SH, P2WPKH (SegWit), P2TR (Taproot)
Spending requires providing an unlocking script (scriptSig/witness) that satisfies the lock
Multi-signature scripts require M-of-N keys to unlock

sections.title: Keys & Addresses
facts: Private key -> Public key -> Address (one-way derivation chain)
Private keys are 256-bit random numbers (2^256 possible keys)
HD wallets derive unlimited key pairs from a single seed phrase
Address formats: 1... (legacy), 3... (P2SH), bc1q... (SegWit), bc1p... (Taproot)

sections.title: Private Keys
facts: A private key is a random 256-bit number
There are more possible private keys than atoms in the observable universe
Private keys must never be shared — possession equals ownership
BIP-39 encodes private key entropy as 12 or 24 human-readable words

sections.title: Public Keys
facts: Public keys are derived from private keys using elliptic curve multiplication (secp256k1)
The derivation is a one-way function — you cannot reverse it to find the private key
Public keys are used to verify signatures without revealing the private key
Bitcoin addresses are hashed public keys with a checksum for error detection

sections.title: Digital Signatures
facts: ECDSA signatures prove ownership without revealing the private key
Every signature is unique even for the same key and message
Taproot introduced Schnorr signatures enabling signature aggregation
Schnorr makes multi-signature transactions indistinguishable from single-signature

sections.title: Consensus & Upgrades
facts: Bitcoin changes through rough consensus — no single entity can force an upgrade
BIP (Bitcoin Improvement Proposal) is the formal process for proposing changes
Soft forks are backwards compatible; hard forks are not
Major upgrades: P2SH (2012), SegWit (2017), Taproot (2021)
SegWit fixed transaction malleability and enabled Lightning Network
Taproot introduced Schnorr signatures, MAST, and Tapscript
Current debates: OP_CAT, OP_CTV, OP_VAULT, BIP 110 (arbitrary data limits)
The Blocksize War (2015-2017) proved users, not companies, control Bitcoin

sections.title: Lightning Network
facts: Lightning is Bitcoin's Layer 2 for instant, near-zero-fee payments
Payment channels allow unlimited off-chain transactions between two parties
HTLCs (Hashed Timelock Contracts) enable trustless multi-hop routing
Lightning capacity exceeds 5,600 BTC with 15,000+ public nodes
Payment success rate exceeds 99%
El Salvador, Steak n Shake, Coinbase, and Cash App support Lightning

sections.title: Am I Too Late?
facts: Bitcoin's market cap is ~0.19% of global store-of-value assets (~$900T)
If Bitcoin captured just 5% of gold's market cap, each BTC would be worth $300K+
Only 21 million BTC will ever exist — currently ~19.7M mined
Network effects suggest adoption follows an S-curve — we are still early on the curve

sections.title: A Bit New Perspective
facts: Technology creates natural deflation — more output per unit of input
Under a Bitcoin standard, the price of goods falls as productivity increases
Jeff Booth's thesis: technology deflates, fiat inflates, Bitcoin lets deflation win
Bitcoin is not just an investment — it's a different measuring stick for value

sections.title: Bitcoin Lore & Milestones
facts: Genesis block (Jan 3 2009) embedded Times headline about bank bailouts
Hal Finney tweeted 'Running bitcoin' on Jan 11 2009
Bitcoin Faucet (2010) gave 5 BTC free per visitor
Casascius physical coins (2011-2013) now sell for $100K+
The actual BTC cap is 20,999,999.9769 — not exactly 21 million
HODL meme originated from a drunken forum typo in December 2013
3-4 million BTC are estimated permanently lost
184 billion BTC were created by a bug in 2010 — Satoshi patched it in 5 hours
Blockstream broadcasts the blockchain from satellites in space

sections.title: Bibliography & Sources
facts: All claims in the site are sourced from primary data: Fed FRED database, BLS CPI data, CBO reports, Bitcoin Core source code, academic papers, and on-chain data
